food_subgroup: Sweetened chilli condiment sauce; bottled commercial product (Lingham's, Maggi); higher sugar and lower heat than sambal oelek

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Singapore-style sweetened chilli sauce (Lingham's / Maggi). Closest parent is generic chili sauce. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chilli sauce — closest defensible FoodEx2 category for bottled sweetened chilli condiment
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (chilli sauce) used as closest parent. No Singapore-specific sweetened chilli sauce code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore chilli sauce condiment.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 tablespoon (18g), the typical amount used as a dipping sauce alongside chicken rice or zi char dishes at a single meal; heavy users may consume 3–5 tablespoons per meal.

nutrient_source.title: Lingham's Chilli Sauce and Maggi Chilli Sauce — Singapore product nutrition labels; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chili sauce (NDB 06021)
nutrient_source.organization: Lingham's Foods / Nestlé Singapore (Maggi); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values derived from Lingham's and Maggi Singapore product labels (energy 120 kcal/100g, carbohydrate 28g, sugar 18g, sodium 1000mg). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central chili sauce (SR Legacy, NDB 06021) for micronutrient profile. Minor micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are estimates consistent with chilli-based condiment composition from USDA SR Legacy. Fat is confirmed zero by product formulation for this category of sweetened chilli sauce.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Chilli Sauce — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweet chilli-based condiments and sugar-containing sauces
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test result exists for Lingham's or Maggi Singapore chilli sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 (medium) is estimated by analogy with sweet chilli-based condiments of similar sugar content (18g/100g predominantly from added sucrose). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 5.04g) / 100 ≈ 3.3. Estimation is consistent with the food's high added-sugar content and moderate starch thickener contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content: 1,000 mg per 100g; 180 mg per tablespoon
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Singapore chilli sauce to no more than 1–2 tablespoons (180–360 mg sodium) per meal, as 3–5 tablespoons delivers 540–900 mg sodium from the condiment alone, which — combined with the sodium in the main dish — can easily push total meal sodium above the 1,500 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar: 18g sugar per 100g; GI ~65 (medium); 3g added sugar per tablespoon
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that 3–5 tablespoons of Singapore chilli sauce adds 9–15g of added sugar (GL ~10–17) per meal from the condiment alone; switching to sambal oelek (zero added sugar, lower GI) is the clinically preferred alternative for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1,000 mg/100g) and high added sugar (18g/100g); negligible fat and zero saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, the primary concerns with Singapore chilli sauce are its very high sodium content — which contributes to hypertension and fluid retention — and its high added-sugar load at typical use quantities; fat and saturated fat are negligible, but portion control is essential to keep meal sodium within cardiovascular-safe limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore chilli sauce (commercially sold as Lingham's Chilli Sauce, Lingham's Extra Hot, or Maggi Chilli Sauce) is the ubiquitous bottled table condiment served at chicken rice stalls, zi char restaurants, hawker centres, and food courts across Singapore and Malaysia. It is a thick, glossy, deep-red sauce with a sweet-tangy-mildly-spicy flavour profile — sweeter and considerably milder than sambal oelek or sriracha, and comparable in sweetness to Western sweet chilli sauce. The sauce is made from chilli, sugar, vinegar, garlic, salt, and starch thickener, and is commercially manufactured and bottled. Per 100g: 120 kcal, 0.5g protein, 0g fat, 28g carbohydrate (18g sugar), 1,000mg sodium. Per standard tablespoon (18g): approximately 22 kcal, 3g sugar, and 180mg sodium. At the typical dipping-sauce usage of 3–5 tablespoons per meal, a diner consumes 540–900mg sodium and 9–15g added sugar from the condiment alone. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the cumulative sugar load from liberal use is clinically significant; sambal oelek (zero added sugar) is the preferred alternative. For hypertension, portion should be limited to 1–2 tablespoons per meal. The sauce is halal, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is the default dipping sauce for Hainanese chicken rice and is also used with roast meats, noodles, and fried foods.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in chilli sauce Singapore
is chilli sauce suitable for diabetics
Singapore chilli sauce GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium) are sourced directly from Lingham's and Maggi Singapore product nutrition labels, which are primary approved sources for packaged commercial products. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chili sauce entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, consistent with the food's sugar composition. Confidence is high for macros and sodium; medium for micronutrients and GI estimate.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — Singapore-style sweetened chilli sauce (Lingham's, Maggi) contains no added fat; this is confirmed zero per product label. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g are all 0.0 by formulation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 confirmed by product label. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based condiment with no animal-derived fat. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a vegan plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a non-fortified condiment with no animal-derived or fortified ingredients. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported on product label and not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singapore chilli sauce formulation. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI test for Lingham's or Maggi Singapore chilli sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GL per serving calculated as (65 × 5.04g carbohydrate per 18g serving) / 100 = 3.3. FoodEx2 code A0FHR used as closest parent for chilli sauce; no Singapore-specific sweetened chilli sauce code exists. FoodOn URI not assigned — no exact match found; closest parent label used.
